item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This skip could work, but it just doesn't. The things are so massive that they could never derive any kind of sustenance from any animal on Earth. It would be like if you could feed from bacteria. Yes it makes for cool imagery, but it doesn't make any internal sense whatsoever.

There are other iffy things in the skip that could be solved with a sweeping rewrite.

Nitpick: You use SCP-2859-1 as if you were referring to a particular instance but then use it to designate the whole species. That's somewhat confusing.

Anyway, downvoted. You can do much more with this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I gave this +1, but I would like to see a bit more about the changes that occurred, as well as some clarification as to what happened that made one of these events kill 600 people.

item.title: Re: Author's Note
item.content:encoded:
I have just a few questions.

1.) How does one view the habits and mating of an 80,000 KM long entity? At the range required to be able to even see all of it, it would be far enough away you would using telescopes. That would be twice as long as this planet is AROUND.

2.) How does a creature that is 80,000 KM in length "eat" a living terrestrial organism? A person is ~2 meters tall, which means we're talking a "mouth" aperture (given the dimensions of snakes' mouths versus their bodies) at least 1000 KM wide. You could put something like 8 Death Stars in a mouth that big side-by-side and still have room left over. For this portal that is opened to be linked to or even in some way directed at the creature's mouth, the portal would have to be 1000 KM wide to direct mouth-sized food toward the creature. That's a very big hole to open between skip cells. Wouldn't any food sent through a portal just be floating through space toward a gaping maw for hours before that maw just… closes with it inside?

3.) How does a creature that is 80,000 KM long derive any energy whatsoever from something at most a few dozen meters in length, ala the largest terrestrial creatures on the planet assuming it can even get at those? (Whales and the like)

I don't mean these questions to be rude or anything. In fact, if they could be even addressed to some extent regarding the size of the creature, this could be a really great skip. I just find it very hard to get into the skip when it's so monstrously huge you can't even conceive of its size, but it eats people and somehow that's enough food for it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For a first I like it. It's short but sweet (Which is something that's mostly in series 1), and, at least for me, encourages you to think about the biology of the snakes, where they come from, etc. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this fundamentally works as just a weird object with no narrative needed. That's rare these days.

I downvoted this mostly because you chose to utilize elision to hide what could be interesting bits. Right now addendums 1 and 2 add nothing and ruin your pacing. It's very possible, if you removed the blackboxes and redactions, that whatever details you're hiding there would actually serve your story, but right now they're meaningless to me. If you removed those addendums entirely the piece would be markedly improved as well.

Either way though, great first effort. Hope to see you on chat so we can see some of your other drafts as they develop.

item.title: Edit
item.content:encoded: Per criticism, size of SCP-2859-1 changed to its original size.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Space snakes? Space snakes. +1

Edit: that being said, 80000km space snakes would be cooler than 30m space snakes, but I don't really know the story behind the downsize.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is very reminiscent of that one scip that's some kind of leviathan that transports people submerged in water to a vast ocean where it devours them. It's effectively the same base concept, except set in space. It seems way too overpowered here, though. Considering the fact that "unused space" is pretty vague terminology, this thing could manifest anywhere, making it impossible to contain and probably very quickly leading to a broken masquerade as a result. As a consequence, it also breaks my suspension of disbelief.

Speaking of the random manifestations, it seems as if the Foundation repeatedly encounters this anomaly in the most conspicuous of locations, raising questions that, remaining unanswered throughout the article, suggest that they were chosen purely in service to the plot. What are the chances that the Foundation first encounters the scip within one of its own facilities? What's the likelihood of a Foundation space mission finding the anomaly at just the right moment in just the right part of space? Unless I'm missing something, it just seems way too contrived.

Another key problem here is that the addenda just seem like a mishmash of different story elements included without any particular rhyme or reason. I mean, why did the one -1 instance morph after eating another SCP? What's the significance of this? How does this relate (if at all) to the apparent cult that has sprung up around the scip? Narrative-wise, there's no cohesion between these individual components, and they all fall flat as a result.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The anomaly manifests in enclosed, unused space (most often between walls) and gradually breaches the object encapsulating it through unknown means.

This is the specific part that got me interested when I skimmed it in chat, and it's still my favorite part in reading the article in full. It's short and succinct sci-fi imagery done right. I feel the same way about the rest of the article's imagery; this is cool as fuck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted for the reasons given in my forum critique - this is so much less interesting than it has the potential to be.

To pick just one example, why did you downsize the serpents from 80,000km long to 30m? They went from awe-inspiring to "eh, like the movie Anaconda", just like that.

item.title: Author's Note
item.content:encoded: This is my first ever SCP and an ambitious one at that, since it's Keter. Whether or not it succeeds, I hope somebody enjoys it!
